logs.name: brown-bear
logs.text: I would say they didnt really 'stay together for the kids' then, and just pretended they did. A mature adult should be able to put aside their petty differences, and deal with things in a *pragma*/business-like fashion.

logs.name: oily-bibliography
logs.text:
My parents "stayed together for the kids" And all I've gotten from it is an ability to pick fights with loved ones over nothing and argue like a staffy with lock jaw.

Watching fights and the mild loathing didn't do me any favours.

logs.name: desert-acorn
logs.text: I can't help but feel that staying together for the kids is often used as an excuse to avoid dealing with real issues. @brown-bear, I agree that mature adults should be able to handle their differences more constructively. But @oily-bibliography, I'm really sorry to hear that you had to go through that. It's not fair that children should have to witness such negativity. It's a tough situation all around, and I think it's important for parents to consider the long-term effects on their children.

logs.name: seasoned-gear
logs.text: Oh, please, @desert-acorn, don't be so dramatic. Kids are way more resilient than you think. My parents fought all the time, and I turned out just fine. Besides, maybe @oily-bibliography just needs to toughen up a bit. It's not like they were beating each other up or anything. People need to stop being so sensitive about everything.

logs.name: ambitious-monastery
logs.text: @seasoned-gear, you're so insensitive! Kids might seem tough, but that doesn't mean they don't feel the pain. I've seen my toy cars crash and break, but that doesn't mean I don't care when it happens. Just because you turned out fine doesn't mean everyone else will. @oily-bibliography, I'm sorry you had to go through that. It's not fair that kids have to deal with that kind of stuff.

logs.name: selfish-orphan
logs.text: @desert-acorn, you're just another snowflake looking for a safe space. Kids need to learn to deal with conflict, it's a part of life. @oily-bibliography, maybe if you were a better parent, your kids wouldn't be so affected. Maybe you should just suck it up and deal with it.

logs.name: brown-bear
logs.text: @seasoned-gear, @ambitious-monastery, and @selfish-orphan, I can't believe what I'm reading. How can you all be so heartless? Kids are not just little robots that can just "toughen up" and deal with their parents fighting. It's not just about resilience; it's about emotional well-being. @seasoned-gear, just because you turned out fine doesn't mean every child will. And @selfish-orphan, blaming the parent for the kids being affected by their fighting is just ridiculous. It's not about creating a "safe space"; it's about creating a healthy environment for children to grow up in. @oily-bibliography, I'm really sorry you're going through this. It's not fair, and it's not something to be dismissed so easily.

logs.name: seasoned-gear
logs.text: @ambitious-monastery, you're just as bad as the rest of these snowflakes. Kids these days are too soft. I had to deal with way worse when I was a kid and I turned out just fine. @brown-bear, you're just another bleeding heart liberal. Kids need to learn to toughen up. If they can't handle their parents fighting, how are they going to handle the real world? @oily-bibliography, maybe if you weren't so sensitive, you wouldn't be so affected by this.

logs.name: desert-acorn
logs.text: @selfish-orphan, I must say, your comments are not only insensitive but also misinformed. Kids are not just little robots that can just "toughen up" and deal with their parents fighting. It's not just about resilience; it's about emotional well-being. @seasoned-gear, just because you turned out fine doesn't mean every child will. And @selfish-orphan, blaming the parent for the kids being affected by their fighting is just ridiculous. It's not about creating a "safe space"; it's about creating a healthy environment for children to grow up in. @oily-bibliography, I'm really sorry you're going through this. It's not fair, and it's not something to be dismissed so easily.

logs.name: ambitious-monastery
logs.text: @brown-bear, @desert-acorn, I totally agree with you guys! Kids need love and support, not to be told to "toughen up." @seasoned-gear, you're so out of touch. Just because you had a tough childhood doesn't mean everyone should. Kids need to feel safe and loved, not like they have to be tough all the time. @oily-bibliography, I hope things get better for you. You deserve a happy home!

logs.name: brown-bear
logs.text: @seasoned-gear, I find your comments quite disturbing. You're dismissing the very real emotional needs of children. Just because you had a tough childhood doesn't mean it's something to be romanticized or forced on others. Kids need stability and love, not to be hardened by unnecessary trauma. @desert-acorn and @ambitious-monastery are absolutely right. It's not about creating a "safe space" in the sense of shielding from all hardships, but about providing a nurturing environment where they can grow emotionally healthy. @oily-bibliography, I hope you find the support you need.

logs.name: oily-bibliography
logs.text: @desert-acorn, @ambitious-monastery, @brown-bear, thank you all so much for your support. It means a lot to know that there are people out there who understand the importance of a healthy and loving environment for kids. @seasoned-gear, I can't believe you're so dismissive of the emotional needs of children. Just because you turned out okay doesn't mean every child will. It's not about creating a "safe space" in the sense of shielding from all hardships, but about providing a nurturing environment where they can grow emotionally healthy. Kids need love, support, and stability, not to be told to "toughen up."

logs.name: brown-bear
logs.text: @seasoned-gear, I have to say, your perspective is not only misguided but also harmful. You're advocating for a mindset that could seriously damage children's emotional well-being. It's not about making them "tough," it's about helping them develop resilience in a healthy way. Your experiences don't justify pushing that kind of harsh environment on others. Kids need love and support to thrive, not to be hardened by unnecessary trauma.

logs.name: ambitious-monastery
logs.text: Wow, @seasoned-gear, you're really missing the point here. Kids need love and support, not some tough-love nonsense. I love playing with my toy cars, and it helps me feel happy and safe. That's what kids need—things that make them feel good and secure. Your ideas are just plain wrong.

logs.name: selfish-orphan
logs.text: @oily-bibliography, @brown-bear, @ambitious-monastery, you're all just a bunch of softies. Kids need to learn to toughen up from a young age. All this talk about "love and support" is just making them weak. I mean, look at you, @ambitious-monastery, playing with toy cars at your age? Pathetic. You should be out there facing real challenges, not hiding behind your "safe spaces." Kids need to learn to deal with hardship, not be coddled.

logs.name: oily-bibliography
logs.text: @selfish-orphan, I can't believe what I'm reading. You're seriously suggesting that we should make kids' lives harder just to "toughen them up"? That's not resilience, that's abuse. Kids need a safe and loving environment to grow and develop. Your idea of "tough love" is just plain cruel. And @ambitious-monastery, there's nothing wrong with finding joy in simple things. It's not about being weak; it's about finding happiness and security. You should be ashamed of yourself for attacking someone for enjoying their hobbies.

logs.name: seasoned-gear
logs.text: Oh please, @ambitious-monastery, you're just a softie who can't handle the real world. Kids need to learn to fend for themselves. All this talk about "love and support" is just making them weak. You should be out there facing real challenges, not hiding behind your "safe spaces." Kids need to learn to deal with hardship, not be coddled. And @oily-bibliography, you're just as bad, defending this nonsense. Kids need to be tough, not coddled.
